font-weight: 700; border-bottom: 2px solid #dee2e6; } table.dict-table td { padding: 5px 10px; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; } Search: Clear Filter by project: MAXED MR21 POWERED ↺ Reset Showing all projects Show: All Variables ✓ Shared Variables Row colours   Variable in all 3 projects   Variable in 2 projects   Variable unique to 1 project ANALOGOUS EXERCISE DECISIONS MR21 1 project (34 variables) The Analogous Exercise Decisions is a lab made measure is a 21 item self-report questionnaire. The measure assesses participants' preferences between exercise and alternative rewards, including monetary values, caloric amounts, and snack portion sizes. ##Scoring### Breakpoints were determined in four categories. In the first category, participants completed 10 items comparing increasing monetary values against 30 minutes of exercise. The breakpoint was defined as the point at which the participant first chose money over exercise. The second category followed the same procedure with 10 items comparing increasing monetary values against 1 hour of exercise, with the breakpoint again identified as the first choice of money over exercise. In the third category, participants completed 5 items in which increasing caloric amounts were presented in the context of how much they would be willing to consume in order to engage in 30 minutes of exercise. The breakpoint was defined as the point at which the participant first indicated they would no longer be willing to consume the specified caloric amount to exercise. The final category included 6 items assessing increasing snack portion sizes using the same format, with the breakpoint identified as the point at which the participant first indicated they would not consume the specified amount in order to engage in 30 minutes of exercise. variables) The Drive for Muscularity Scale is a 15 item questionnaire that assesses the perceptions and behaviors surrounding the desire to gain muscles (McCreary & Sasse, 2000). Questions are rated on a Likert scale from 1 (Always) to 6 (Never). The items are then reversed scored and higher sum scores indicating higher drive for muscularity. Scoring 1. Selects only variables that are relevant for the current measure Items are re-coded to be reversed coded ("1=5, 2=4, 3=3, 4=2, 5=1, 6=0") Creates a symptom average score, which gives an average count of the number of symptoms (0-5) that are present for each individual Key Variables muscularity_average (mean score of all items) VariableLabelValueProjectIn Projects muscularity_wishI wish that I were EATING DISORDER EXAMINATION MAXEDMR21POWERED All 3 projects (370 variables) Eating disorder pathology was assessed using the Eating Disorder Examination (EDE; Fairburn & Cooper, 1993), a structured interview that assesses the frequency and severity of eating disorder symptoms over the past three months. Four subscales are present in the measure (Restraint, Eating Concern, Shape Concern, and Weight Concern) as well as a global score that is the mean of the four subscales. The EDE has been shown to have good reliability and validity in both clinical and non-clinical samples. Test–retest reliability coefficients for the four subscale scores ranged from 0.50 to 0.88 (Berg et al., 2012). Subscales Restraint — attempts to limit food intake Eating Concern — preoccupation with eating, food, or calories Shape Concern — dissatisfaction with body shape Weight Concern — preoccupation with weight and desire to lose weight Scoring Most items are rated on a 0–6 scale. Subscale scores are the mean of their items; the global score is the mean of the four subscales. Higher scores indicate greater pathology. Key Variables EDE_global — global score of the EDE, mean of the four subscales EDE_restraint_mean — mean of the restraint subscale items EDE_eating_concern_mean — mean of the eating concern subscale items EDE_shape_concern_mean — mean of the shape concern subscale items EDE_weight_concern_mean — mean of the weight concern subscale items Fairburn, C. G., & Cooper, Z. (1993). The Eating Disorder Examination. Berg, K. C., Peterson, C. B., Frazier, P., & Crow, S. J. (2012). Psychometric evaluation of the eating disorder examination and eating disorder examination-questionnaire: a systematic review of the literature. obesity scoresNAMR21MAXEDMR21 epsi_muscle_buildEPSI Sum of muscle building scoresNAMR21MAXEDMR21 ED 100K PLUS POWERED 1 project (307 variables) The ED100k-Plus is a lab adapted version of the Ed100k. It is a self report measure that assesses current and history of an eating disorder and includes items from the Dieting and Weight History Questionnaire ( variables start with wdqhq in measure), ED History, Eating Disorder Examination-Questionnaire, . It includes questions about binge eating, compensatory behaviors, restriction and other eating disorder symptoms. This measure is not yet validated. Scoring Diagnostic variables are scored per the DSM-5 criteria for threshold and sub-clinical (past and current) Anorexia Nervosa, Atypical Anorexia Nervosa Bulimia Nervosa, Binge Eating Disorder. Diagnostic variable are rated on 0,1 scale where 0 = no diagnosis and 1 = diagnosis. Key Variables dx_an_current_fem_plus- Current Anorexia Nervosa diagnosis for females dx_an_history_fem_plus - History of Anorexia Nervosa diagnosis for females dx_atyp_an_current_plus - Current Atypical Anorexia Nervosa diagnosis dx_atyp_an_history_plus - History of Atypical Anorexia Nervosa dx_bn_history_plus - History of Bulimia Nervosa dx_bn_current_plus - Current Bulimia Nervosa diagnosis dx_bn_nopurge_hist_plus - History of Bulimia Nervosa (No Purging) dx_bn_nopurge_cur_plus - Current Bulimia Nervosa (No Purging) dx_bed_history_plus - History of Binge Eating Disorder diagnosis dx_bed_current_plus - Current Binge Eating Disorder diagnosis dx_sub_bed_history_plus - History of Subthreshold Binge Eating Disorder diagnosis dx_sub_bed_current_plus - Current Subthreshold Binge Eating Disorder diagnosis dx_purging_history_plus - History of Purging Disorder diagnosis dx_purging_current_plus - Current Purging Disorder diagnosis VariableLabelValueProjectIn Projects ed_100k_plus_timestampSurvey TimestampNAPOWEREDPOWERED ed100kp_dem_plusDEMOGRAPHICSNAPOWEREDPOWERED ed100kp_age_plusCurrent ageNAPOWEREDPOWERED ed100kp_sex_plusSex assigned at birth?NAPOWEREDPOWERED ed100kp_cur_weight_plusCURRENT WEIGHT AND HEIGHTNAPOWEREDPOWERED ed100k_lbs_kg_plusThe following section will ask about how your weight has changed over the years. Day? Month?" Patient Score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_2"Where are we now? State? County? Town/city? Street? Address?" Patient Score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_3The examiner names three unrelated objects (ex. penny, lamp, dog) clearly and slowly, then the instructor asks the patient to name all three of them. The patient's response is used for scoring. The examiner repeats them until patient learns all of them, if possible. Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_4"I would like you to count backward from 100 by sevens." (93, 86, 79, 72, 65, ...) Alternative: "Spell WORLD backwards." (D-L-R-O-W) Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_5"Earlier I told you the names of three things. Can you tell me what those were?" Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_6Show the patient two simple objects, such as a wristwatch and a pencil, and ask the patient to name them. Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_7"Repeat the phrase: 'No ifs, ands, or buts.'" Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_8"Take the paper in your right hand, fold it in half, and put it on the floor." (The examiner gives the patient a piece of blank paper.) Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_9"Please read this and do what it says." (Written instruction is "Close your eyes.") Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_10"Make up and write a sentence about anything." (This sentence must contain a noun and a verb.) Patient score:NAMR21MR21POWERED mmse_11"Please copy this picture." (The examiner gives the patient a blank piece of paper and asks him/her to draw the symbol below. All 10 angles must be present and two must intersect.) rest_yesnoHas there ever been a time when you a) tried to limit the amount of food you ate to influence your weight, b) tried to exclude foods from your diet to influence your weight, and/or c) tried to follow rules (such as following a calorie limit) to influence your weight?NAPOWEREDPOWERED rest_startageHow old were you when you first engaged in these behaviors to influence your weight?NAPOWEREDPOWERED rest_endageHow old were you when you last engaged in these behaviors to influence your weight?NAPOWEREDPOWERED rest_directionsNow, on a timeline, mark the age at which the participant first engaged in restriction and when they last engaged in restriction. Mark 6 month increments along the timeline. For each 6 month increment, assess whether the participant was engaging in clinical, subclinical, or no restriction. Criteria: Did you... 1. Deliberately try to limit the overall amount of food you ate? [No = the participant denies attempting to decrease the amount of food they were eating Subclinical = the participant attempted to reduce the amount of food/calories they were eating for a reason other than weight loss, was following a medically-prescribed eating plan in which caloric or food restriction was not drastic, or reduced the amount of food or calories they were eating, but the reduction was not drastic (< 300 calorie reduction from baseline) Clinical = the participant attempted to reduce the amount of food/calories they were eating drastically (> than 300 calorie reduction from baseline)] 2. Try to exclude food that you liked from your diet? [No = the participant denies excluding any food they liked from their diet Subclinical = the participant stopped eating 1-2 foods or excluded foods for reasons other than weight loss Clinical = the participant stopped eating more than 2 foods or excluded entire food groups with the goal of losing weight] 3. Try to follow rules related to food, such as calorie counting or cutting out food groups? [No = the participant denies following rules related to food Subclinical = the participant read food labels, considered the calories in food before eating it, or followed rules for medical reasons rather than weight loss Clinical = the participant followed a calorie limit, tracked food in an app, or followed rules that significantly limit the amount or types of food they could consume for the purpose of losing weight] 4. Skip meals? [No = the participant denies skipping meals (i.e., consistently ate 3 meals per day whenever possible) Subclinical = the participant skipped meals for reasons other than weight loss (not hungry in the morning, lack of time), skipped meals less than twice per week, or skipped meals to "save calories" for other meals] Clinical = the participant skipped meals more than 3 times per week for the rest_directions_42bccfNow, on a timeline, mark the age at which the participant first engaged in restriction and when they last engaged in restriction. Mark 6 month increments along the timeline. For each 6 month increment, assess whether the participant was engaging in clinical, subclinical, or no restriction. Criteria: Did you... 1. Deliberately try to limit the overall amount of food you ate? [No = the participant denies attempting to decrease the amount of food they were eating Subclinical = the participant attempted to reduce the amount of food/calories they were eating for a reason other than weight loss, was following a medically-prescribed eating plan in which caloric or food restriction was not drastic, or reduced the amount of food or calories they were eating, but the reduction was not drastic (< 300 calorie reduction from baseline) Clinical = the participant attempted to reduce the amount of food/calories they were eating drastically (> than 300 calorie reduction from baseline)] 2. Try to exclude food that you liked from your diet? [No = the participant denies excluding any food they liked from their diet Subclinical = the participant stopped eating 1-2 foods or excluded foods for reasons other than weight loss Clinical = the participant stopped eating more than 2 foods or excluded entire food groups with the goal of losing weight] 3. Try to follow rules related to food, such as calorie counting or cutting out food groups? [No = the participant denies following rules related to food Subclinical = the participant read food labels, considered the calories in food before eating it, or followed rules for medical reasons rather than weight loss Clinical = the participant followed a calorie limit, tracked food in an app, or followed rules that significantly limit the amount or types of food they could consume for the purpose of losing weight] 4. Skip meals? [No = the participant denies skipping meals (i.e., consistently ate 3 meals per day whenever possible) Subclinical = the participant skipped meals for reasons other than weight loss (not hungry in the morning, lack of time), skipped meals less than twice per week, or skipped meals to "save calories" for other meals] Clinical = the participant skipped meals more than 3 times per week for the Overview This codebook documents variables from three studies — POWERED, MAXED, and MR21 — grouped into six conceptual categories, with a seventh Administrative / Other appendix for REDCap system fields (timestamps, record IDs, form completion flags, consent/signature fields, etc.). Each variable is tagged with the project(s) in which it appears: POWERED MAXED MR21 Each chapter below has one searchable, sortable, filterable table. Use the column filters at the top of each table to narrow by Measure or Project.
Chapter 3 Demographics Age, sex assigned at birth, gender identity, pronouns, race/ethnicity, education, household characteristics, and other participant background variables.
Chapter 4 Anthropometric Measures Height, weight, BMI, Tanner staging, and pubertal development indicators.
Chapter 5 Medical History Medical diagnoses, surgeries, hospitalizations, medications, menstrual history, substance use, vitals, and prior weight history.
Chapter 6 Mental Health Diagnosis Diagnostic interviews and standardized symptom measures — DIAMOND, BDI-II, PHQ-9, GAD-7, RCADS, YBOCS, OCI, Columbia SSRS, DSM-5 Cross-Cutting Symptoms, MMSE.

Chapter 7 Eating Disorder Behaviors EDE, EPSI, ED100K / ED100K+, ED History, Drive for Thinness/Muscularity, Compulsive Exercise, Exercise Dependence, ART, Goldfarb Fear of Fat, Analogous Exercise Decisions, SRHI (habitual eating), post-interview target-weight survey, and binge / purge / restrict / food-intake / milkshake task items from the visit forms.

Chapter 8 Socio-behavioral Anxiety, mood, emotion regulation, perfectionism, social evaluation, body image state, interoception, and other transdiagnostic trait / state measures — ASI, BIS/BAS, DERS, Frost MPS, IUS, Self-Compassion, SATAQ, BFNE, Perceptions of Teasing, SPSRQ, FADE/FAMB, NVS, PVS, EMA, OCYC Maladaptive, Body Appreciation.

Administrative / Other REDCap system fields and study-flow variables: survey timestamps, record IDs, form-complete flags, consent/signature fields, visit start/end times, payment records, and interviewer notes. Included for completeness; not substantive measures.

Notes & caveats Mixed intake forms (DCF, DCF-Parents, Study Visit A/B/C Collection Forms) are split heuristically; review entries flagged in Administrative / Other to confirm none should be promoted to a substantive category. Duplicate variable names across projects are collapsed into a single row with all source projects listed as badges. If the same name refers to different constructs in different projects, split them in codebook.csv before rendering. Value coding is reproduced from the source REDCap export and truncated to 180 chars in the category tables — full strings are preserved in codebook.csv. ART (Athletes’ Relationship with Training) is placed under Eating Disorder Behaviors because of its use in assessing compulsive exercise in ED populations. Move it to Socio-behavioral if you prefer a behavioral-trait framing. ASI is treated as the Anxiety Sensitivity Index (socio-behavioral), not an eating-symptom inventory. Tanner Stages / PDS are treated as anthropometric/maturation indicators. Move them in codebook.csv if your analyses treat them as medical history. To refine categorization, edit codebook.csv (hand-correct the category column) and re-render.

Chapter 10 Final Words We have finished a nice book.
